one_line_takeaway: A chunk-and-vote (de)randomized smoothing scheme gives the first deterministic, model-agnostic certified robustness for raw-byte malware detectors against patch AND content-insertion attacks, dramatically outperforming randomized-smoothing baselines on BODMAS while remaining vulnerable to very large (>=100% file size) benign-content injections.
key_finding: The chunk-based CS detectors are substantially more robust to content-insertion attacks than both undefended and randomized-smoothing baselines, and their certified accuracy often exceeds baselines' empirical adversarial accuracy. Examples from the paper's own tables: Shift attack at 4096 bytes drops NS-MalConv to 35.60% adversarial accuracy while CS-MalConv (z=512) holds 96.60% (certified 96.60%); Code-caves attack drops NS-MalConv to 22.20% and RS-MalConv to 25.20% while CS-MalConv reaches 96.00% (certified 90.40%); GAMMA (10 sections) drops NS-MalConv to 26.20% and RS-MalConv to 24.20% while CS-MalConv reaches 96.20% (certified 93.40%); the combined attack drops NS-MalConv to 33.00% while CS-MalConv holds 85.20% (certified 81.60%). Certified accuracy decays with payload/patch size and reaches 0 when a patch equals 50% of the file or an inserted payload equals 100% of the file; smaller chunk sizes give higher certified robustness because more chunks must be flipped. CS is also cheaper at inference than RS/RsDel (e.g. RS-MalConvGCT 1.4038 s/example CPU vs CS-MalConvGCT 0.5537 s).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a chunk-based (de)randomized smoothing scheme that is a DETERMINISTIC, model-agnostic certified defense for end-to-end (raw-byte) deep-learning malware detectors against functionality-preserving evasion attacks. A base classifier is trained on a single randomly-selected contiguous chunk of z bytes; at inference the executable is split into non-overlapping z-byte chunks, each classified independently, and the file label is the plurality (majority) vote. Because the number of ablated versions is finite and enumerable, the smoothed classifier can be computed exactly, yielding deterministic certificates (in contrast to the probabilistic certificates of randomized-smoothing baselines). The paper's own novel additions over the authors' prior workshop paper [16] are: (1) a PREPROCESS step that zero-pads PE headers and sections to a multiple of the chunk size so that inserted bytes are confined to whole chunks, which extends the certificate from patch/append attacks to content-INJECTION attacks (shift, GAMMA, code-caves); (2) evaluation across three architectures (MalConv, MalConvGCT, AvastConv); and (3) a finer-grained interpretability/visualization use of per-chunk scores. NOTE: randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., Gibert et al. RS, Huang et al. RS-Del) and (de)randomized smoothing for images (Levine & Feizi) are CITED prior work the paper builds on and compares against, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: White-box-aware but evaluated as black-box, functionality-preserving evasion: an attacker holds a known-malicious Windows PE and injects or manipulates bytes so the detector labels it benign, without breaking the executable's functionality. Manipulations are confined to locations that preserve behavior: slack space between sections (Slack patch attack), appended overlay bytes (Padding), space injected before/after sections (Shift), newly created benign-content sections (GAMMA), and dynamically extended code caves. Adversarial payloads are optimized with a genetic algorithm (population 10, 50 steps, 30% mutation, soft labels) to convert white-box attacks into black-box ones. The certificate assumes the adversary controls a bounded region: a patch of size p (bytes replaced) or an inserted payload of size p, expressed as a percentage of the original file. The defense guarantees no adversarial EXEmple exists for a malicious file if the malware-chunk count exceeds the benign-chunk count by the required margin (2Delta for patch, Delta for insertion, Delta = ceil(p/z)+1).
limitations: 1) The defense is fundamentally beatable by a knowledgeable attacker: injecting a benign payload equal to or larger than 100% of the original file size can flip the majority vote, so detection accuracy collapses at that regime (the authors state and demonstrate this as a key vulnerability, Fig. 7). 2) Certified accuracy provably reaches 0 at patch=50% / insertion=100% of file size. 3) Clean accuracy of smoothed models is ~2% lower than undefended models, and per-chunk base accuracy is lower still (mitigated only by voting). 4) Restricting the base classifier to a single chunk prevents learning global PE-structure features. 5) Evaluation is limited to BODMAS PE files <=1MB, so scalability to large binaries is untested. 6) The genetic-algorithm attack is weaker than gradient-based FGSM (authors note this), so empirical robustness may be optimistic, though the certificate itself is worst-case. 7) The non-negative-network baseline was dropped for lack of reproducible weights/code. 8) Larger chunk sizes weaken robustness, forcing a robustness/accuracy trade-off.

datasets_used: BODMAS PE malware dataset (full: 57,293 malware across 581 families + 77,142 benign Windows PE, Aug 2019-Sep 2020)
BODMAS subset restricted to files <=1MB: 39,360 benign + 37,742 malicious PE files, temporal train/val/test split
500-malware sub-test set used for adversarial-attack evaluation
quantitative_results: Shift attack (4096 bytes): NS-MalConv 35.60% adversarial accuracy vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 96.60% (certified 96.60%); NS-AvastConv 31.20% vs CS-AvastConv (z=1024) 94.20% (certified 93.80%)
Code-caves attack: NS-MalConv 22.20%, RS-MalConv 25.20%, RsDel-MalConv 68.60% vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 96.00% (certified 90.40%)
GAMMA attack (10 sections): NS-MalConv 26.20%, RS-MalConv 24.20%, RsDel-MalConv 91.80% vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 96.20% (certified 93.40%)
Padding+Slack (10000 bytes): NS-MalConv 85.00%, RS-MalConv 90.00%, RsDel-MalConv 73.80% vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 95.80% (certified 95.40%)
Combined attack: NS-MalConv 33.00% vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 85.20% (certified 81.60%); NS-AvastConv 31.20% vs CS-AvastConv (z=1024) 79.20% (certified 73.00%)
Certified accuracy vs patch size for CS-MalConv (z=512): 95.36% at p=1%, 88.21% at p=20%, 62.49% at p=40%, 0.0% at p=50%
Certified accuracy vs insertion payload for CS-MalConv (z=512): 95.83% at p=1%, 91.57% at p=30%, 65.08% at p=80%, 0.0% at p=100%
Clean accuracy: undefended NS models ~2% higher than smoothed; e.g. test-set accuracy NS-AvastConv 98.69%, CS-AvastConv (z=512) 96.24%; NS-MalConv 96.96% vs CS-MalConv (z=512) 96.06%
Inference time (CPU s/example): NS-MalConv 0.0132, RS-MalConvGCT 1.4038, RsDel-MalConvGCT 1.3270, CS-MalConvGCT (z=2048) 0.5537, CS-MalConv (z=512) 0.0543
Chunk-size effect (GAMMA, CS-MalConvGCT): z=512 gives 94.60% (certified 92.00%) vs z=2048 gives 93.00% (certified 84.40%) - smaller chunks are more robust
baselines_compared: RS - randomized ablation smoothing of Gibert et al. [13] (20% byte-ablation, N=20)
RsDel - randomized deletion smoothing RS-Del of Huang et al. [14] (3% deletion, N=20)
Attacks as evaluation baselines: Slack/Padding [8], Shift [9], GAMMA [10], Code-caves [11], and a combined attack
